definitions.definition: A market that facilitates the transfer of funds from those who have surplus (households) to those who need them (businesses), through various financial instruments.
Market for short-term funds with maturity up to 1 year. Deals in near-cash instruments. Low risk, high liquidity.
Market for long-term funds with maturity more than 1 year. Deals in shares, debentures, bonds. Higher risk than money market.
Company issues new securities directly to investors (IPO/FPO). Funds go to the company. Price fixed by the company.
Investors buy and sell existing securities among themselves through stock exchanges. Funds go to the selling investor, not the company.
Short-term government borrowing instrument issued by RBI. Available in 91, 182, and 364 day maturities. Sold at discount, redeemed at par.
Unsecured promissory note issued by large creditworthy companies for short-term borrowing. Maturity: 15 days to 1 year.
Short-term loans between banks that can be recalled at any time (1 to 15 days). The interest rate is called 'call rate'.
An organised market for buying and selling existing securities. Provides liquidity, price discovery, safety, and acts as an economic barometer.
Dematerialised account that holds securities in electronic form. Required for trading in the stock exchange.
Securities and Exchange Board of India. Established 1988, statutory powers 1992. Protects investors, regulates intermediaries, develops the securities market.
Initial Public Offering — when a company offers its shares to the public for the first time to raise capital.

points.a: New securities issued for first time

scenarios.scenario: RBI issues 91-day bills at a discount to manage short-term government borrowing.
scenarios.principle: Short-term money market instrument issued by government

scenarios.scenario: Tata Motors announces an IPO, offering 10 crore new shares to the public at ₹500 each.
scenarios.principle: Company issues new securities — funds go to the company

scenarios.scenario: An investor sells 100 Reliance shares to another investor through BSE at the current market price.
scenarios.principle: Existing securities traded — funds go to the seller, not the company

scenarios.scenario: A large company issues an unsecured promissory note to borrow funds for 90 days.
SBI lends ₹500 crore to HDFC Bank overnight, which HDFC repays the next morning.
scenarios.principle: Very short-term loans between banks — can be recalled anytime

scenarios.scenario: SEBI bans a company director from trading after discovering he bought shares using confidential earnings data.
scenarios.answer: SEBI Protective Function: Prohibiting Insider Trading
scenarios.principle: Preventing unfair profit from confidential information

scenarios.scenario: Share prices on BSE and NSE crash after negative GDP growth data is released.
scenarios.principle: Rise/fall in prices reflects economic health

scenarios.scenario: An investor opens a Demat account, selects a broker, places a buy order online, and receives shares in T+1 settlement.
scenarios.principle: Modern electronic trading and settlement process

scenarios.scenario: SEBI allows internet trading and promotes training of intermediaries to develop the market.
A bank issues negotiable short-term deposits to corporate investors at competitive interest rates.
scenarios.principle: Money market instrument issued by banks
